points: India's population of 1.21 billion (17.5% of world population) is unevenly distributed: half lives in five states (UP, Maharashtra, Bihar, West Bengal, Andhra Pradesh); density varies from 1,102/sq km (Bihar) to 17/sq km (Arunachal Pradesh); variations caused by terrain, climate, rainfall, and soil fertility.

India overtook China in 2023 to become world's most populous country despite only 2.4% of world's land area; population density among world's highest after Bangladesh and Japan; uneven distribution reflects geographical and socio-economic disparities across regions.

summary.category: Population Growth and Processes of Change
points: Population increased from 361 million (1951) to 1,210.6 million (2011); growth rate peaked at 2.22% (1981) then declined to 1.64% (2011) due to falling birth rates; paradox: despite declining rate, absolute additions increase yearly because low rate applied to huge base population (182 million added in 1990s alone).

Three processes drive change: birth rates (higher than death rates historically), death rates (rapidly declined through healthcare improvements), migration (internal: rural-to-urban; external: immigration-emigration); natural increase = birth rate minus death rate; till 1980 high births + declining deaths caused rapid growth; since 1981 both declining.

summary.category: Population Structure and National Population Policy
points: Population characteristics: age structure (young population, significant below 15 years), dependency ratio (dependents vs economically active), sex ratio (females per 1000 males—varies regionally), literacy rate (correlates with development and fertility decline).

National Population Policy 2000: development-driven approach focusing on free compulsory education (age 14), reducing infant mortality (below 30/1000), universal immunisation, delayed marriage promotion, people-centred welfare; recognises social development naturally leads to demographic transition and lower fertility rather than direct population control mandates.
